segments.inferno-11-g76: “E io:” is an elliptical speech tag: the verb of saying is omitted, so it means “And I [said].” This is a common narrative formula in Dante’s dialogue.
segments.inferno-11-g77: “Maestro” is a vocative, directly addressing Virgil as Dante’s guide and teacher. It stands outside the syntax of the following clause.
segments.inferno-11-g78: The subject “la tua ragione” follows the verb “procede,” a poetic/inverted word order: literally, “your explanation proceeds.” “Chiara” is feminine singular agreeing with “ragione,” and “assai” intensifies it, meaning “very clear.”
segments.inferno-11-g79: The verb “distingue” is coordinated with “procede” and has the same subject, “la tua ragione.” “’l” is the contracted form of “il,” and “ch’e’ possiede” is a relative clause: “che” refers to “popol,” while archaic “e’” functions as a pronoun meaning “it,” referring back to the abyss that holds or contains the people.
segments.inferno-11-g80: “Dimmi” is the imperative “di’” plus the enclitic pronoun “mi”: “tell me.” “Ma” marks Dante’s objection or follow-up question after accepting Virgil’s explanation.
segments.inferno-11-g81: “Quei” is a substantivized demonstrative meaning “those people,” introducing a list of groups punished outside the city of Dis. “De la” is the older separated form of modern “della,” and “pingue” describes the marsh as thick, greasy, or slimy.
segments.inferno-11-g82: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to another implied group of souls: “those whom the wind drives.” The word order is inverted: the object “che” comes first, while “il vento” is the subject of “mena.”
segments.inferno-11-g83: This is another coordinated relative clause parallel to “che mena il vento.” Again, “che” is the object, “la pioggia” is the subject, and “batte” is present indicative: “whom the rain beats.”
segments.inferno-11-g84: This final relative clause is coordinated with the previous ones. “S’incontran” is an apocopated poetic form of “s’incontrano,” and the reflexive pronoun here is reciprocal, “meet/clash with one another”; “con sì aspre lingue” means with such harsh speech or tongues.
segments.inferno-11-g85: “Perché” introduces the main question, “why?” after the long list of groups. “Sono ei puniti” has poetic inversion: “ei” is the subject “they,” and “sono puniti” is a passive construction; “dentro da la città roggia” means inside the red/fiery city, with “da la” as an older form of “dalla.”
segments.inferno-11-g86: “Se” here means “if,” though that sense is not represented in the supplied list. “Li” is a direct-object pronoun, “them,” and “ha in ira” is an idiom meaning “has in anger,” that is, “holds in wrath” or “is angry with.”
segments.inferno-11-g87: This clause balances the previous conditional: “if God holds them in wrath” versus “if he does not.” The object pronoun “li” again means “them,” and the object complement “in ira” is understood from the preceding sentence.
segments.inferno-11-g88: “Perché” again asks “why?” and introduces the consequence Dante finds puzzling. “A tal foggia” is an idiomatic phrase meaning “in such a manner/condition,” with “tal” modifying “foggia.”
segments.inferno-11-g89: This segment contains only punctuation, marking the close of Dante’s quoted question.
segments.inferno-11-g20: “Figliuol mio” is a vocative address: Virgil calls Dante “my son.” “Figliuol” is an archaic or poetic form of “figliuolo,” with “mio” following the noun as is common in Italian.
segments.inferno-11-g21: “Dentro da cotesti sassi” gives the location of the three circles: inside or within the rocky barrier near Dante. “Cotesti” is a demonstrative referring to something near the person addressed, and “sassi” here means not small stones but rocky cliffs or masses of rock.
segments.inferno-11-g22: This is the narrative frame interrupting the direct speech: “he then began to say.” “Cominciò” is passato remoto, the normal narrative past tense in Dante; “a dir” is the infinitive “a dire” with final vowel dropped poetically.
segments.inferno-11-g23: “Son” is the contracted form of “sono,” with the subject “tre cerchietti” following the verb. “Di grado in grado” literally means “from step to step” or “level by level,” describing the circles as arranged successively downward.
segments.inferno-11-g24: “Come que’ che lassi” means “like those that you leave,” where “que’” stands for “quelli,” referring back to circles already passed. “Che” is a relative pronoun, and “lassi” is an archaic second-person singular present form meaning “you leave.”
segments.inferno-11-g25: “Tutti” refers to all the three little circles just mentioned. “Son pien” is poetic for “sono pieni,” with “pien” shortened before its complement “di spirti maladetti,” meaning “full of accursed souls.”
segments.inferno-11-g26: Here “perché” is purposive, meaning “so that,” and it introduces a subjunctive clause: “ti basti” means “may suffice for you.” “Pur” has the limiting sense “only, merely,” so the phrase means that later the sight alone will be enough.
segments.inferno-11-g27: “Intendi” is an imperative addressed to Dante: “understand.” The paired indirect questions “come e perché” depend on it, while “son costretti” is a passive construction meaning “they are constrained/confined” in their assigned places.
segments.inferno-11-g28: “D’ogne malizia” is a fronted genitive phrase, literally “of every wickedness,” setting up the topic whose “fine,” or aim, will be stated later. “Ogne” is an archaic form of “ogni.”
segments.inferno-11-g29: This relative clause modifies “malizia”: it is wickedness “which earns hatred in Heaven.” “Ch’” is elided before the vowel of “odio,” and “in cielo” means in Heaven, that is, in God’s judgment.
segments.inferno-11-g30: This is the main clause: “ingiuria è ’l fine,” with “’l” a contracted form of “il.” “Fine” here means not simply an ending but an aim or purpose: the purpose of malice is injury or wrong.
segments.inferno-11-g31: “Ogne fin cotale” means “every such aim,” with “fin” as the shortened form of “fine.” The paired “o ... o ...” means “either ... or ...,” and “altrui contrista” says that such an aim afflicts another person or others, whether by violence or by fraud.
segments.inferno-11-g32: “Perché” is causal here: “because.” “De l’uom” is the separated older spelling of “dell’uomo,” and “proprio” means “proper to” or characteristic of man; the clause says that fraud is a specifically human evil, since it abuses reason.
segments.inferno-11-g33: “Più spiace a Dio” means “it displeases God more,” with the subject still being “frode.” “Spiace” is third-person singular present, and “a Dio” marks the person to whom the thing is displeasing.
segments.inferno-11-g34: “Però” has its old strong sense “therefore,” drawing the consequence from fraud’s greater guilt. “Stan” is a shortened form of “stanno,” and “di sotto” is an adverbial phrase meaning “below”; “li frodolenti” uses archaic “li” for modern “i,” meaning “the fraudulent.”
segments.inferno-11-g35: Here “dolor” is the subject and “li” is an object pronoun meaning “them,” referring to the fraudulent, though the supplied lemma lists it as an article. The verb “assale” is singular because its subject is singular: “greater pain assails them.”
segments.inferno-11-g1: “In su” works together as a poetic prepositional phrase meaning “upon” or “on top of.” The relative clause “che facevan gran pietre rotte in cerchio” has the subject after the verb: literally, “which great broken stones made in a circle,” meaning that the rocks formed the rim or edge.
segments.inferno-11-g2: “Venimmo” is first-person plural passato remoto, the narrative past: “we came.” “Sopra più crudele stipa” links to the preceding location and means they arrived above a harsher, more cruelly crowded or tangled place below.
segments.inferno-11-g3: “E quivi” simply carries the narrative forward: “and there.” The adverb “quivi” is literary/archaic for “there,” referring to the place just reached.
segments.inferno-11-g4: This is a causal prepositional phrase: “because of / on account of the horrible excess of the stench.” The relative clause “che ’l profondo abisso gitta” has “abisso” as subject and “gitta” as present-tense verb: the abyss “casts” or “throws up” the stench.
segments.inferno-11-g5: “Ci raccostammo” is reflexive: literally “we brought ourselves near,” hence “we drew near” or “we moved close.” The verb is passato remoto, continuing the main past-tense narration.
segments.inferno-11-g6: “In dietro” is an adverbial phrase meaning “backward” or “back.” It modifies “ci raccostammo,” explaining that they drew near while moving back from the foul smell.
segments.inferno-11-g7: “Ad un coperchio” gives the goal of their movement: they drew back “to” or “up to” a lid. “D’un grand’ avello” depends on “coperchio,” specifying that it is the lid of a large tomb; “grand’” is elided before the vowel in “avello.”
segments.inferno-11-g8: “Ov’” is an elided form of “ove,” meaning “where.” “Dicea” is an archaic/imperfect form of “diceva,” here used of writing or an inscription: it “said” or “read.”
segments.inferno-11-g9: This begins the inscription itself. “Guardo” is first-person singular present, with the tomb speaking as if it were a person: “I guard/hold Anastasius the pope.”
segments.inferno-11-g10: “Lo qual” is an archaic relative pronoun phrase meaning “whom,” referring back to Pope Anastasius. The word order is inverted: “Fotin” is the subject of “trasse,” while “lo qual” is the object, so Photinus drew Anastasius away from the straight path.
segments.inferno-11-g11: “Lo nostro scender” uses the infinitive “scender” as a noun: “our descending,” or more naturally “our descent.” “Conviene esser tardo” means “it is necessary/fitting to be slow,” with “esser tardo” as the infinitive complement of “conviene.”
segments.inferno-11-g12: “Sì che” introduces purpose or result: “so that.” “S’ausi” is a subjunctive form with reflexive “si,” meaning “may accustom itself”; the subject comes later, “il senso,” and “al tristo fiato” gives what the sense must become accustomed to.
segments.inferno-11-g13: “Poi” means “afterward,” contrasting with “in prima” in the previous segment. “Fia” is an archaic future form of “essere,” “will be,” and “no” stands for “non”; the small “i” is an old dative/locative particle, roughly “to it / in it,” not the article.
segments.inferno-11-g14: “Così ’l maestro” is elliptical: the verb “spoke/said” is understood from context, so it means “Thus the master [spoke].” “’l” is the elided form of “il.”
segments.inferno-11-g15: “E io” introduces Dante’s reply, with the reporting verb delayed until the next segment. “Alcun compenso” is the object of the coming imperative “trova”: he asks Virgil to find some compensating use of the time.
segments.inferno-11-g16: “Dissi” is passato remoto, “I said.” “Lui” is used as an indirect object without the modern preposition “a,” so the sense is “I said to him.”
segments.inferno-11-g17: “Trova” is an imperative addressed to Virgil: “find.” The clause “che ’l tempo non passi perduto” means “so that the time may not pass wasted”; “passi” is present subjunctive after “che,” and “perduto” is a participle describing the time as lost or wasted.
segments.inferno-11-g18: “Ed elli” introduces Virgil’s reply: “And he.” “Elli” is an old form of “egli,” meaning “he,” not related here to the listed meanings of “air” or “sky.”
segments.inferno-11-g19: “Vedi” is second-person singular present, here meaning “you see” or “you understand.” “Ch’a ciò penso” contracts “che a” and uses “pensare a” in the sense “to think of/about,” with “ciò” referring to Dante’s request for a way not to waste the time.
segments.inferno-11-g90: “Ed elli a me” means “And he to me,” with “elli” an archaic/poetic form for “egli,” ‘he’; “a me” marks the addressee. “Perché” opens a direct question, and “delira” is present indicative, used of the intellect ‘wandering’ or ‘straying’ from its proper course.
segments.inferno-11-g91: “Disse” is the passato remoto of “dire,” a narrative past tense: “he said.” It interrupts the quoted question in a common reporting construction.
segments.inferno-11-g92: “Lo ’ngegno tuo” is “your intellect,” with “’ngegno” an apheresized poetic form of “ingegno” after the article. “Da quel che sòle” means “from what it is accustomed to do”: “da” marks departure, “quel che” means “that which/what,” and “sòle” is an archaic present form of “suole,” ‘is wont,’ with the expected action left implicit.
segments.inferno-11-g93: “O ver” functions like “ovvero,” ‘or else/or rather,’ not as the adjective “true.” “La mente” is the subject of “mira,” present indicative, while “dove altrove” gives the idea of looking toward some other place or object.
segments.inferno-11-g94: “Non ti rimembra di...” is an archaic construction meaning “Do you not remember...,” with “ti” functioning as the person affected by the remembering. “Con le quai” is old Italian for “con le quali,” referring back to “parole,” and “la tua Etica” is the subject of “pertratta.” “Disposizion” is a shortened poetic plural, and “che ’l ciel non vole” is a relative clause; “’l” is contracted “il,” and “vole” is archaic for “vuole.”
segments.inferno-11-g95: “Incontenenza” begins the appositive list of the three “disposizion.” In the Aristotelian moral context, it means lack of self-control or incontinence.
segments.inferno-11-g96: “Malizia” and “la matta bestialitade” complete the list. “La” is the article before “bestialitade,” “matta” is an adjective placed before the noun for emphasis, and “bestialitade” is an archaic form corresponding to modern “bestialità.”
segments.inferno-11-g97: This segment continues the preceding question with “e come,” ‘and how.’ “Incontenenza” is the subject of both verbs, “offende” and “accatta,” while “men” modifies each verb phrase: it offends God less and acquires or incurs less blame.
segments.inferno-11-g125: Da queste due means “from these two,” referring back to the two sources just mentioned: nature and art. The feminine plural queste is used substantively, with due understood as “two things/sources.”
segments.inferno-11-g126: Se introduces a conditional clause: “if you bring to mind.” Ti rechi is present subjunctive/indicative-looking in an idiom recarsi a mente, “to recall to mind”; lo Genesì is an archaic form for “Genesis,” and dal principio means “from the beginning.”
segments.inferno-11-g127: Convene is impersonal: “it is necessary / one must.” The infinitives prender and avanzar depend on it; sua vita means the life or livelihood proper to humankind, and la gente means people generally, “mankind.”
segments.inferno-11-g128: E perché gives the cause for the following judgment: “and because.” L’usuriere is the singular representative “the usurer”; tene is archaic for tiene and, with via, means “follows/takes a way.”
segments.inferno-11-g129: The object natura is placed before the verb dispregia for emphasis: “he scorns nature.” Per sé and per la sua seguace mean “in herself” and “in her follower,” the follower being human art/industry, which imitates nature.
segments.inferno-11-g130: Poi ch’ forms the causal conjunction poi che, “since/because.” Pon is a shortened form of pone, “places,” and pon la spene means “sets his hope,” with spene an archaic poetic form of speranza, “hope.”
segments.inferno-11-g131: Ma marks a turn from explanation back to action. Seguimi is an imperative with the enclitic pronoun mi, “follow me”; che is causal here, and ’l gir is elided il gir, a substantivized infinitive meaning “the going,” so mi piace means “is pleasing to me / I wish.”
segments.inferno-11-g132: Ché is causal, “for/because,” explaining why they must move on. I Pesci names the zodiac sign Pisces, and guizzan is a poetic shortened plural for guizzano, “dart”; su per l’orizzonta means upward along or over the horizon, with orizzonta an archaic form of orizzonte.
segments.inferno-11-g133: ’L Carro is elided il Carro, “the Wain,” the constellation also known as the Great Bear/Big Dipper. Tutto emphasizes the whole constellation, and giace here means “lies/is situated”; Coro is not “choir” here but the northwestern quarter or wind, Caurus.
segments.inferno-11-g134: ’L balzo means “the rocky ledge” or bank of the circle. Si dismonta is an impersonal/passive construction, “one descends / it is descended,” while via là oltra points to the place farther along, “over there beyond.”
segments.inferno-11-g36: The phrase places the complement Di vïolenti before the subject for emphasis: literally, “of violent sinners the first circle is wholly.” tutto functions adverbially/predicatively, meaning that the circle is entirely made up of, or assigned to, such sinners.
segments.inferno-11-g37: ma perché introduces the reason for the subdivision: “but because.” si fa is an impersonal construction, “one does/commits,” and far forza a means “to do violence to/against”; tre persone anticipates the three objects named in the next sentence.
segments.inferno-11-g38: in tre gironi means “into three rings,” with in marking division into parts. è distinto e costrutto uses essere with past participles in a passive/predicative sense: the circle “is divided and arranged/constructed.”
segments.inferno-11-g39: A Dio is the first of three parallel indirect objects governed by far forza later in the sentence. The preposition a marks the target against whom violence is directed.
segments.inferno-11-g40: a sé is the second parallel object, “to/against oneself.” sé is the stressed reflexive pronoun and refers generically to any person, not to a particular named subject.
segments.inferno-11-g41: al prossimo completes the threefold list: God, self, neighbor. si pòne far forza is an impersonal modal expression, effectively “one can do violence,” with the infinitive phrase far forza depending on the impersonal verb.
segments.inferno-11-g42: dico introduces a clarification: “I mean.” in loro e in lor cose specifies the two ways violence can touch these targets, in their persons and in their things/possessions; lor is the shortened poetic form of loro, here possessive.
segments.inferno-11-g43: come udirai looks forward to the explanation that follows, with the future udirai, “you will hear/learn.” con aperta ragione means “with clear explanation,” where aperta has the figurative sense “clear, evident.”
segments.inferno-11-g44: Morte per forza e ferute dogliose are the compound subject: “death by violence and painful wounds.” nel prossimo marks the person harmed, while si danno is a passive/impersonal construction meaning “are given/inflicted,” though danno is the verb form here rather than the noun “damage.”
segments.inferno-11-g45: e nel suo avere shifts from harm to the neighbor’s person to harm to his possessions. avere is used substantively, “property/possessions,” and ruine begins the list of damages done to property.
segments.inferno-11-g46: incendi e tollette dannose continues the list of injuries to property. tollette is an archaic plural meaning robberies or plunderings, and dannose modifies it as “harmful/destructive.”
segments.inferno-11-g47: onde is not the noun “waves” here but a connective meaning “wherefore/therefore,” drawing the consequence from the crimes just named. ciascun che mal fiere means “everyone who wounds wickedly”; fiere is an archaic verb form from ferire, not the adjective “fierce.”
segments.inferno-11-g48: guastatori e predon adds two more classes of violent sinners: destroyers/ravagers and robbers. predon is a shortened poetic plural form corresponding to predoni.
segments.inferno-11-g49: The word order is inverted: lo giron primo is the subject, tormenta is the verb, and tutti is the object. per diverse schiere means that the sinners are punished in different groups or ranks within that first ring.
segments.inferno-11-g50: Puote omo is an archaic order and form for “a man/person can.” avere in sé man vïolenta literally means “have a violent hand against himself,” i.e. commit violence against oneself; ne’ suoi beni extends that violence to one’s own possessions.
segments.inferno-11-g51: e però introduces the consequence: such sinners belong in the second ring. convien che ... si penta means “it is necessary that ... repent,” with penta in the subjunctive after convien che; sanza pro means their repentance is “without benefit,” too late to help them. qualunque priva sé del vostro mondo means “whoever deprives himself of your world,” that is, of earthly life.
segments.inferno-11-g52: biscazza is used verbally here, “gambles away,” although the form is related to gambling. fonde literally suggests “pours out” and figuratively means squanders; la sua facultade means “his means/resources.”
segments.inferno-11-g53: e piange continues the relative clause governed by qualunque: the same sinner “weeps.” là dov’ is a contraction of là dove, “there where”; esser de’ means “ought to be,” with de’ standing for dee/deve, and giocondo is the adjective “joyful,” not the noun “gambler.”
segments.inferno-11-g54: “Puossi” is an archaic contraction of “può si,” an impersonal form meaning “one can.” “Far forza” is an idiom meaning “do violence,” and “ne la” is the separated poetic form of “nella,” here governing “deïtade,” the Godhead or divine nature.
segments.inferno-11-g55: The gerunds “negando” and “bestemmiando” explain how this violence against the divine is done. “Col cor” means “with/in the heart,” and “quella” refers back to “deïtade.”
segments.inferno-11-g56: This segment continues the chain of gerunds with “spregiando,” coordinated with the previous “negando” and “bestemmiando.” “Sua” refers to “natura,” so the phrase means scorning Nature and Nature’s goodness or bounty.
segments.inferno-11-g57: “E però” introduces the conclusion: “and therefore.” The subject is “lo minor giron,” with apocopated poetic forms “minor” and “giron”; “suggella del segno suo” means “marks/seals with its own sign,” and “Soddoma” and “Caorsa” are metonymic names for sodomy and usury.
segments.inferno-11-g58: This participial phrase modifies “chi”: the person is characterized as speaking while inwardly scorning God. “Col cor” again means “with/in the heart,” emphasizing inward contempt rather than merely outward speech.
segments.inferno-11-g59: “Favella” is a finite verb here, third-person singular present, meaning “speaks.” It completes the relative construction begun by “chi”: “whoever ... speaks.”
segments.inferno-11-g60: “La frode” is placed first as the object of “usare” later in the sentence. The article “La” gives it a general, categorical sense: fraud as a type of sin.
segments.inferno-11-g61: “Ond’” means “whereby” or “from which,” introducing a relative clause explaining the moral effect of fraud. “È morsa” is a passive construction: every conscience is “bitten,” that is, tormented or stung by it.
segments.inferno-11-g62: The normal order would be “l’omo può usare la frode,” but Dante places the object earlier and lets the subject follow the modal verb. “Che ’n lui fida” means “who trusts in him,” while “fidanza non imborsa” is a metaphor for someone who does not store up or place trust in him.
segments.inferno-11-g63: “Questo modo di retro” means “this latter mode,” referring back to fraud used against someone who does not trust. “Par ch’incida” uses “par” plus a subjunctive, “incida,” and “pur” has the restrictive sense “only”; “lo vinco d’amor” means the bond of love, though the supplied sense list for “vinco” does not contain that meaning.
segments.inferno-11-g64: “Onde” here is a connective meaning “therefore” or “wherefore,” not the noun “waves.” “S’annida” is reflexive in form, literally “nests itself,” with the following list of sins as the subject placed after the verb.
segments.inferno-11-g65: This segment continues the postposed compound subject of “s’annida.” “Chi affattura” means “whoever bewitches” or “those who practice enchantment,” coordinated with “lusinghe.”
segments.inferno-11-g66: “Falsità” is another item in the list of sins lodged in this circle. As a singular abstract noun, it has a general sense: falsehood or deceit as a class of wrongdoing.
segments.inferno-11-g67: This segment adds two more coordinated nouns to the same list. “Ladroneccio” denotes robbery or thievery, and “simonia” is the buying and selling of sacred things or offices.
segments.inferno-11-g68: “Ruffian” is a plural poetic form, referring to panders or pimps. It continues the list without a repeated conjunction before the next item.
segments.inferno-11-g69: “Baratti” here means fraudulent dealing or barratry, not innocent barter. “E simile lordura” closes the catalogue broadly: “and similar moral filth.”
segments.inferno-11-g70: “Per l’altro modo” means “by/through the other mode,” namely fraud against one who trusts. “S’oblia” is passive-reflexive in sense, “is forgotten” or “is neglected,” and its subject is “quell’amor che fa natura,” the love that Nature creates.
segments.inferno-11-g71: “Quel” stands for another kind of love or bond, parallel to “quell’amor” in the previous segment. “Ch’è poi aggiunto” is a relative clause with passive “è aggiunto,” meaning “which is afterward added.”
segments.inferno-11-g72: “Di che” means “from which,” referring to the added bond just mentioned. “Si cria” is a passive-reflexive form meaning “is created,” and “fede spezïal” means special trust or special faithfulness.
segments.inferno-11-g73: Here “onde” means “therefore,” introducing the consequence for the worse form of fraud. “Nel cerchio minore” means “in the smaller/smallest circle,” the innermost circle of Hell.
segments.inferno-11-g74: “Ov’” is elided “ove,” meaning “where.” The phrase “in su che” functions as “upon which”: the point of the universe is the place upon which Dis sits.
segments.inferno-11-g75: “Qualunque trade” means “whoever betrays”; “trade” is an archaic form related to “tradire.” “È consunto” is a passive construction, and “in etterno” means “forever” or “eternally.”
segments.inferno-11-g98: This opens a conditional clause: “Se” means “if,” followed by the present subjunctive/indicative-looking form “riguardi” addressed to “tu.” “Ben” intensifies the verb, and “questa sentenza” refers back to the doctrinal statement just discussed, meaning a maxim or authoritative judgment rather than merely a grammatical sentence.
segments.inferno-11-g99: “Rechiti” is an imperative-like form with enclitic “ti”: literally “bring to yourself,” hence “recall to mind.” The indirect question “chi son quelli che...” depends on this remembering; “sù di fuor” means “up above, outside” the present lower circle, and “sostegnon” is an archaic plural form of “sostengono,” here “undergo.”
segments.inferno-11-g100: The main clause follows the condition: “tu vedrai ben” means “you will clearly understand.” “Perché ... sien dipartiti” is an indirect question with the subjunctive “sien,” and “da questi felli” means “from these wicked ones,” referring to the more grievous sinners below.
segments.inferno-11-g101: This second “perché” coordinates another indirect question with the previous one: “and why...” The word order is poetic: the subject is “la divina vendetta,” while “li” is the object pronoun “them,” and “martelli” is a verb meaning “hammers/strikes,” with “men crucciata” meaning “less angered” or “less severe.”
segments.inferno-11-g102: Dante addresses Virgil metaphorically as a “sun,” a source of intellectual light; the listed lemma for “sol” is misleading here, since the word means “sun.” “Che” introduces a relative clause, and “sani” is a verb, “you heal,” governing “ogne vista turbata,” every troubled or clouded power of sight.
segments.inferno-11-g103: The clause “tu mi contenti” means “you satisfy me,” with “mi” as the direct object. “Sì ... che” forms a result construction: “you satisfy me so much when you solve/explain...”
segments.inferno-11-g104: This “che” completes the result construction begun by “sì”: “so ... that.” It introduces the consequence of Virgil’s satisfying explanations.
segments.inferno-11-g105: “Non men che” is a comparative phrase meaning “no less than.” “Saver” is an apocopated infinitive used substantively, meaning “knowing” or “knowledge.”
segments.inferno-11-g106: “Dubbiar” is another apocopated infinitive used as a noun: “doubting.” “M’aggrata” is “mi aggrata,” meaning “pleases me”; the elided “m’” is a pronoun, not the preposition suggested by the supplied lemma.
segments.inferno-11-g107: Dante asks Virgil to go back in the argument: “ancora in dietro” means “still/further back” or “once more backward.” “Ti rivolvi” is a second-person imperative in sense, with the reflexive pronoun “ti”: “turn yourself back.”
segments.inferno-11-g108: “Diss’ io” is an inverted reporting phrase, with “diss’” standing for “dissi,” “I said.” The subject “io” follows the verb, a common poetic and narrative order.
segments.inferno-11-g109: “Là dove” means “there where,” pointing to the specific point in Virgil’s earlier explanation. “Di’” is the shortened form of “dici,” “you say,” followed by “ch’” introducing the object clause: “that usury offends divine goodness.”
segments.inferno-11-g110: “’l” is the elided article “il.” “Groppo” is literally a knot and here a metaphor for an intellectual difficulty; “solvi” continues the same metaphor, “untie” or “resolve” it.
segments.inferno-11-g111: “Filosofia” is the subject of the later verb “nota,” meaning “makes known” or “shows.” Dante personifies Philosophy as an authority that teaches the relation between God, Nature, and human art.
segments.inferno-11-g112: This is the reporting clause: “mi disse,” “he said to me.” The dative pronoun “mi” precedes the past-tense verb “disse,” as normal in Italian.
segments.inferno-11-g113: “A chi la ’ntende” means “to whoever understands her/it,” with “chi” used indefinitely. “La” is a pronoun referring back to Philosophy, and “’ntende” is an elided form of “intende.”
segments.inferno-11-g114: Here “nota” is not a noun but a finite verb from “notare”: “marks out,” “indicates,” or “makes known.” It governs the following indirect clause beginning with “come.”
segments.inferno-11-g115: “Non pure” in this older usage means “not merely” or “not only.” “In una sola parte” means “in one single place/section,” implying that philosophy teaches this doctrine in more than one passage.
segments.inferno-11-g116: “Come” introduces the content of what Philosophy shows: “how Nature takes her course.” The phrase “dal divino ’ntelletto e da sua arte” gives the source from which Nature derives her order: the divine Intellect and His creative art.
segments.inferno-11-g117: This begins a conditional clause: “and if you note your Physics well.” “Fisica” refers to Aristotle’s Physics, a work of natural philosophy; “note” is a verb form meaning “observe” or “take note of,” not the adjective “known.”
segments.inferno-11-g118: “Tu troverai” is the main clause following the condition: “you will find.” The future tense presents the conclusion as something Dante will discover by consulting the cited text.
segments.inferno-11-g119: “Non dopo molte carte” literally means “not after many pages,” that is, not far on in the book. “Carte” here means pages or leaves of a manuscript/book.
segments.inferno-11-g120: “Che” introduces the object clause after “troverai.” “L’arte vostra” means human art or craft, and “quella” refers back to “natura,” Nature, which human art imitates.
segments.inferno-11-g121: “Quanto pote” means “as much as it can.” “Pote” is an archaic form of “può,” not the verb suggested by the supplied lemma.
segments.inferno-11-g122: “Segue” is the main verb of the clause introduced in the preceding segments: human art “follows” Nature. The word carries the idea of imitation and discipleship.
segments.inferno-11-g123: This simile clarifies the relationship: human art follows Nature as a learner follows a master. “Fa” is elliptical, meaning “does,” i.e. “does follow,” with the object “’l maestro” placed before “’l discente” in poetic order.
segments.inferno-11-g124: “Sì che” introduces the result: since human art follows Nature, and Nature derives from God, human art is metaphorically God’s grandchild. “Quasi” marks the expression as figurative, and “nepote” here has the older sense “grandchild/descendant.”

sentences.inferno-11-s17: And I: “Master, your explanation proceeds very clearly, and very well distinguishes this abyss and the people it holds.”
sentences.inferno-11-s18: But tell me: those of the greasy marsh, those whom the wind drives, and those whom the rain beats, and those who meet one another with such harsh tongues—why are they not punished inside the red city, if God holds them in wrath?
sentences.inferno-11-s19: And if he does not hold them in wrath, why are they in such a condition?”
sentences.inferno-11-s5: “My son, within these rocky cliffs,” he then began to say, “there are three little circles, level below level, like those you are leaving.”
sentences.inferno-11-s6: They are all full of accursed spirits; but so that afterward mere sight may be enough for you, understand how and why they are confined.
sentences.inferno-11-s7: Of every wickedness that earns hatred in Heaven, injury is the aim; and every such aim afflicts others either by violence or by fraud.
sentences.inferno-11-s8: But because fraud is an evil proper to man, it displeases God more; and therefore the fraudulent are down below, and greater pain assails them.
sentences.inferno-11-s1: Upon the edge of a high bank, which great broken rocks formed in a circle, we came above a more cruel thicket; and there, because of the horrible excess of the stench that the deep abyss casts up, we drew back close to the lid of a great tomb, where I saw an inscription that said: “I hold Pope Anastasius, whom Photinus drew from the straight path.”
sentences.inferno-11-s2: “Our descent must be slow, so that the sense may first grow a little accustomed to the wretched breath; and afterward it will be no concern.”
sentences.inferno-11-s3: Thus the master; and I said to him, “Find some compensation, so that the time may not pass wasted.”
sentences.inferno-11-s4: And he said: “You see that I am thinking of that.”
sentences.inferno-11-s21: And he to me: “Why does your intellect stray so far,” he said, “from what it is wont to do?”
sentences.inferno-11-s22: or does your mind look elsewhere?
sentences.inferno-11-s23: Do you not remember those words with which your Ethics treats fully the three dispositions that Heaven does not will: incontinence, wickedness, and mad bestiality?
sentences.inferno-11-s24: and how incontinence offends God less and incurs less blame?
sentences.inferno-11-s29: From these two, if you bring Genesis to mind from its beginning, humankind must derive its life and advance; and because the usurer follows another way, he scorns nature both in herself and in her follower, since he sets his hope on something else.
sentences.inferno-11-s30: But follow me now, for it pleases me to go on; for Pisces are darting up over the horizon, and the Wain lies wholly over Caurus, and the cliff is descended farther on over there.
sentences.inferno-11-s9: The first circle is entirely for the violent; but because violence can be done against three persons, it is divided and arranged in three rings.
sentences.inferno-11-s10: Violence may be done to God, to oneself, and to one’s neighbor—I mean against their persons and their possessions—as you will hear by a clear explanation.
sentences.inferno-11-s11: Death by violence and painful wounds are inflicted on one’s neighbor, and on his possessions ruin, fires, and harmful plunder; therefore murderers and everyone who maliciously wounds, ravagers and robbers, the first ring torments them all in separate bands.
sentences.inferno-11-s12: A person can lay violent hands on himself and on his own goods; and therefore in the second ring anyone who deprives himself of your world, gambles away and squanders his means, and weeps there where he ought to be joyful, must repent without profit.
sentences.inferno-11-s13: One can do violence to the Godhead by denying and blaspheming it in one's heart, and by scorning Nature and her goodness; and therefore the smallest ring marks with its own sign Sodom, Cahors, and whoever, scorning God in his heart, speaks.
sentences.inferno-11-s14: Fraud, by which every conscience is stung, a person may use against one who trusts him and against one who does not pocket trust.
sentences.inferno-11-s15: This latter mode seems to sever only the bond of love that Nature makes; hence in the second circle nest hypocrisy, flatteries and those who bewitch, falsehood, theft and simony, panders, barratry, and similar filth.
sentences.inferno-11-s16: By the other mode, that love which Nature makes is forgotten, and also that which is afterward added, from which special trust is created; therefore in the smallest circle, where the point of the universe is on which Dis sits, whoever betrays is consumed forever.
sentences.inferno-11-s25: If you consider this maxim carefully, and recall to mind who those are who up above, outside, undergo punishment, you will clearly see why they are separated from these wicked ones, and why divine retribution hammers them less wrathfully.
sentences.inferno-11-s26: “O sun who heals every clouded sight, you satisfy me so when you resolve things that doubting pleases me no less than knowing.
sentences.inferno-11-s27: “Turn back a little further,” I said, “to where you say that usury offends divine goodness, and untie the knot.”
sentences.inferno-11-s28: “Philosophy,” he said to me, “makes clear to whoever understands her, not in one place only, how Nature takes her course from the divine Intellect and from His art; and if you note your Physics well, you will find, not many pages later, that your art follows that one as far as it can, as the learner follows the master; so that your art is, as it were, God’s grandchild.
